title: Commemorating the 100th anniversary of the city of Hialeah, Florida.
plain_summary: This bill is a special announcement celebrating the 100th birthday of Hialeah, Florida. Hialeah was started in 1925 and now has more than 236,000 people living there. The city has played important roles in airplane history, making things during World War Two, and is home to many Cuban Americans. The bill recognizes how Hialeah has helped make Florida and America better through its economy and culture.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

sections.title: Resolution Commemorating the 100th Anniversary of the City of Hialeah, Florida
sections.text: The House of Representatives recognizes that the city of Hialeah was created as an official city in nineteen twenty-five with one thousand five hundred people living there. Now more than two hundred thirty-six thousand people call it home. The city gets its name from a Seminole Indian phrase that means high prairie. Glenn Curtiss, who was a pioneer in aviation, and James Bright, who raised cattle, founded the city. In nineteen twenty-five, the Hialeah Park Racetrack opened and was called The Grand Dame. Famous people like Winston Churchill visited there. In nineteen thirty-seven, Amelia Earhart announced at the old Miami Municipal Airport that she planned to fly around the world. She had her airplane repaired in Hialeah and took off from there on June first. During World War Two in nineteen forty-three, Hialeah became an important place for making things the military needed, including uniforms and airplane parts. In nineteen sixty-one, Rene Sedanos opened a small grocery store called Sedanos in East Hialeah. This store grew into a chain that now has thirty-four locations across Florida. In nineteen seventy-two, the Cao family opened the first Vicky Bakery in East Hialeah and it became an important part of the community. In nineteen seventy-three, a band called KC and the Sunshine Band was started in a small studio in East Hialeah by Harry Wayne Casey, who grew up in Hialeah. The band became very successful in the seventies and eighties with five number one hit songs and sold over one hundred million records around the world. In nineteen seventy-four, Bernard Cap company was started in Hialeah and still makes high quality hats, clothing, and accessories for military forces, police, airlines, and other organizations after more than fifty years. In nineteen eighty-four, Gus Machado bought an old car dealership on Forty-ninth Street in Hialeah and later made it the largest Hispanic-owned Ford dealership in Florida. Hialeah is home to hard-working families who work every day toward their American dream. The community is a special mix of different nationalities and cultures. For many years, Hialeah has been home to thousands of Cuban people who had to leave their country because of the harsh government there. In nineteen ninety-three, Lincoln Diaz-Balart became a member of Congress and was the first Cuban American to represent Hialeah in the House of Representatives. He represented Hialeah until he retired in twenty eleven, and then his brother Mario Diaz-Balart took over and proudly represents Hialeah today. Today, Hialeah is the fifth largest city in Florida and the sixth largest municipality. Ninety-five percent of the people who live there identify as Hispanic or Latino, which is one of the highest percentages in the country. About eighty-four point one percent of Hialeah residents have Cuban backgrounds, the highest proportion in the United States. The city is known for its strong industries that help Florida's economy. Hialeah is one of the largest employers in Miami-Dade County. The city contributes to Florida's economy, culture, and history through its diverse and lively community. Therefore, the House of Representatives congratulates the city of Hialeah on its one hundredth anniversary. The House recognizes and appreciates how much the city of Hialeah has contributed economically and culturally to South Florida and the entire United States. The House also honors the people of Hialeah for their unique and important contributions to the success, culture, and history of the United States.
